segments.s306-g129: بداندیش را (object + را) places 'the malicious/ill‑minded one' as the definite object. از میان‌بر کردن is expressed here with کنَم (کنم) — an optative/subjunctive/intentional verb form meaning 'I will do / I will cause'; literally 'to cut out/from the midst.' Word order is object/phrase + verb, typical of Persian (SOV with omitted subject).
segments.s306-g130: سر بدنشان را is a noun phrase 'the head of their body/heads' marked by را as definite object. بی‌افسر is an adjectival phrase meaning 'without a helmet/crest' and follows the noun it qualifies; کنم (here again first‑person subjunctive/intentional) applies to the whole phrase: 'I will render their heads helmetless.'
segments.s306-g131: سخن هرچه گفتم groups 'sokhan' (words/speech) with the relative 'harche goftam' ('whatever I said'), forming 'whatever words I said.' نیا را بگوی uses را to mark the indirect object 'to Niyâ' and بگوی (present/subjunctive/imperative form of گفتن) as the directive 'tell (him/her/it).' The verb bگوی addresses the addressee (you) to convey those words.
segments.s306-g132: که introduces a subordinate clause giving an additional command ('and/that'), followed by the locative phrase در جنگ 'in battle.' چندین بهانه is the object 'many/several excuses/pretexts' placed before the verb. مجوی is an archaic/poetic negative jussive/imperative (with negative prefix م‑) meaning 'do not seek' (addressing the listener).
segments.s306-g97: Word order is inverted for emphasis: the verb گفت (past 'said') appears after the adverb همین ('this/same') and before the subject names. هم (before جمشید) is the simple additive 'also/too'. This clause therefore reads literally 'this said Zahhak and also Jamshid' — i.e., 'Zahhak said the same, and Jamshid too.'
segments.s306-g98: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شدشان is شد + شان ('became/was to them' — 'their ... became'), so the verb is in the past. دل ('heart') is the inner state; از + نیکویی expresses being 'from/of goodness' (i.e. losing hope in goodness). ناامید is a predicate adjective 'hopeless' describing دل. Together: 'when their hearts became hopeless of goodness.'
segments.s306-g99: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). ما را uses را as the definite object/emphasis marker after the pronoun 'us/our'. دل ابلیس بی‌راه کرد is literal 'the heart the Devil made off the road' — i.e. 'the Devil led the heart of/of us astray.' کرد is the past tense 'did/caused.'
segments.s306-g100: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from/of'. هر نیکویی = 'every goodness'. دست کوتاه کرد is an idiomatic causative phrase: دست = 'hand/power/help' and کوتاه کردن = 'to shorten' → 'to deprive (one) of power/help.' کرد is the past verb 'did/made.' So 'he deprived (us) of every goodness.'
segments.s306-g101: نه negates the clause: برگشت here means a 'turning' or 'reversal/withdrawal'. از ایشان = 'from them' (i.e. away from them). بد روزگار = 'ill fate' or 'bad times.' So literally 'It was not that bad fate turned away from them' → 'ill fate did not leave them.'
segments.s306-g102: ز = 'from'; بد گوهر = 'bad essence/character' (گوهر as 'essence'). و + گفت آموزگار: و = 'and', گفت here functions as the speech verb 'said' introducing the teacher's remark, and آموزگار = 'teacher/guide.' (Note: the provided sense set for گفت does not list the verbal sense 'to say' so that sense is not indexed below.) The whole: 'From a bad essence — said the teacher' (i.e. the teacher said, 'ill fortune proceeds from bad nature').
segments.s306-g103: کسی = 'someone/whoever'. In classical/poetic Persian, کو after a noun often functions as a relative ('who/whom'); here it marks the protasis 'whoever'. بتابد (subjunctive/present archaic form of تابیدن) is used as 'turns/turns away' (sense 'turn/bend'), so بتابد سر از راستی = 'turns his head from righteousness' = 'turns away from the right/path of truth.'
segments.s306-g104: گزیند (present/habitual form) = 'chooses' (third-person). همی is an adverb (here 'ever/always/indeed') intensifying the verb. کژی و کاستی = 'crookedness and deficiency/shortcoming' — the things chosen as opposite to راستی. So: 'will choose crookedness and defect.'
segments.s306-g41: This is a simple nominal + verbal phrase in archaic/poetic Persian: 'زننده' is an agent noun 'the one who strikes', followed by the enclitic/adverbial 'همی' marking continuous or habitual action, then the object 'تازیانه' and the present/past-poetic verb form 'زند' (here meaning 'strikes' / 'keeps striking'). Word order is agent — adverbial — object — verb, typical in classical Persian narration.
segments.s306-g42: This is a subordinate purpose/result clause introduced by 'که تا' (classical combination meaning roughly 'so that/in order that'). 'دخترش' is the possessor phrase 'his daughter', 'بچه را' is the definite object marked by 'را', and 'بفگند' is a subjunctive/optative classical verb form expressing the intended/resultant action (here glossed as 'may cast/throw'). The clause gives the aim or result of the lashing.
segments.s306-g43: 'خردمند پیران' is a compound nominal phrase meaning 'the wise of the elders' or 'the wise elder(s)'; 'بدانجا' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to that place', and 'رسید' is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached'. Subject — locative — past verb is a straightforward narrative order.
segments.s306-g44: 'بدید' is the past-tense verb 'saw'. It is followed by the relative/complement phrase 'آن‌که هرگز ندید و شنید' — literally 'that which he never saw and heard' — where 'آن‌که' (= 'that which/what') fronts the content and the verbs 'ندید' and 'شنید' are past perfective negatives 'had never seen' and 'had never heard'.
segments.s306-g45: 'چنین بود فرمان یزدان' is an emphatic copular clause 'thus was the command of God' (چنین = 'thus', بود = past 'was', فرمان یزدان = 'the command/decree of God'). The following 'که من' begins the subordinate clause reporting the content of that command.
segments.s306-g46: 'سرافراز گردم' is a predicative phrase where 'سرافراز' is an adjective 'exalted/honored' and 'گردم' is a present-subjunctive/optative form of 'گشتن' meaning 'that I become' or 'I should be'. The prepositional phrase 'به هر انجمن' ('in/at every assembly') completes the idea: the command was that I should be exalted everywhere.
segments.s306-g47: The phrase 'گزند و بلای تو' is the compound subject 'your harm and your calamity'; 'از من بگاشت' is a past-tense transitive construction meaning 'he laid/caused (them) to be from me' or 'he put (the blame/affliction) on me', with 'از' marking source/attribution and 'بگاشت' the past-tense verb 'placed/assigned'.
segments.s306-g48: This is an explanatory clause introduced by 'که' ('that/for'). 'با من زمانه' ('time/fate with me') places 'زمانه' (here 'time' or 'fortune' as a personified force) together with the speaker; 'یکی راز داشت' literally 'had one secret' — i.e. 'time shared a secret with me'. Word order 'با من زمانه' is topical and 'داشت' is the past tense 'had'.
segments.s306-g49: از آن پس که ... is a temporal clause meaning “since that time when…”. گشتم is the past intransitive verb (گشتن) “I became/was” and is followed by the phrase ز مادر جدا “separated from my mother”; Persian here uses the preposition ز (poetic ز) + noun to mark the source of separation. Word order: temporal introducer → verb → source phrase.
segments.s306-g50: چنانچون بود introduces a simile or comparison: چنانچون “just as/so” + بود (past of بودن) links to the nominal phrase بچه بینوا “a wretched child”. The copula بود places the predicate after it in a slightly elevated/poetic order: “I was (like) a poor child.”
segments.s306-g51: به پیش + group (شبانان) means “to the presence of / to the shepherds.” فرستادیم is the past 1st-person‑plural form “we sent”; in epic narrative plurals often represent the acting group (those who decided or acted). The direct object (مرا) is omitted here but understood from context.
segments.s306-g52: This clause has the verb دادیم “we gave” (past 1st‑pl.). به پروار is a prepositional phrase meaning literally “to/for the fattened/well‑grown,” and شیران نر is a descriptive phrase where شیران is used metaphorically for brave/strong men (“lions”) and نر specifies masculine; together the phrase says they entrusted/gave the child to robust/brave males for rearing. Note the line’s compressed, poetic phrasing allows some ambiguity (literal “fattened lions” vs. figurative “powerful men”).
segments.s306-g53: مرا is the object marker “me” (written here as م + را in the word list); the noun phrase دایه و پیشکاره شبان is a coordinated description “a nurse and a servant/attendant of the shepherds.” پیشکاره is an older word for an attendant/maidservant (also can mean courtesan in other contexts) and is here best read as “attendant.” Word order: object (مرا) precedes the compound predicative phrase.
segments.s306-g54: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative construction “neither … nor …”. آرام روز = “the day’s peace/rest” and خواب شبان = “the shepherd’s sleep” (i.e. sleep at night); together they mean there was no rest by day and no sleep by night. The nouns stand in noun‑noun compounds typical of poetic diction.
segments.s306-g55: چنین بود تا introduces a continuing state “it was thus until …”. روز من (literally “my day”) is an idiomatic phrase meaning my opportunity/turn or my fortunes, and برگذشت (past of برگذشتن) means “passed by/passed.” The clause as a whole: “It remained so until my day passed.”
segments.s306-g56: مرا (again written as م + را in the list) is the direct object “me.” اندر آورد is the past tense compound “brought in / introduced” (آورد “brought” with the older preposition اندر “into/in”). پیران ز دشت is the agent phrase “the elders from the plain.” Word order places the object before the verb for emphasis: “the elders from the plain brought me in.”
segments.s306-g73: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. دیدی is 2nd-person past 'you saw' and introduces a subordinate clause. بر و گردگاه ورا is the noun phrase seen by دیدی: بر here functions as the prepositional element 'on/upon', گردگاه 'neck/area around the pivot' is the head noun, and ورا is a demonstrative/enclitic pointing 'to/at it' that follows the noun (a typical classical/poetic postposed deictic).
segments.s306-g74: This is a paratactic list of three qualities applied to the same referent (the thing indicated by ورا). بزرگی 'greatness/size' and گردی 'roundness' are coordinated by و, followed by another و plus راه 'the way/path'; the final ورا again points those qualities 'to it'. The nouns follow Persian order (head then modifiers/particles).
segments.s306-g75: بجنبیدت is a past (classical/poetic) verbal form meaning 'moved/stirred' (the verb form appears with a bound ending as often in older Persian poetry; here it denotes the jewel's motion). آن گوهر بد ز جای is the noun phrase 'that bad/evil jewel from its place' where ز جای means 'from the place/its place'.
segments.s306-g76: بیفگندی is past of افکندن 'to throw/cast' (3rd‑person past in poetic usage) — 'it threw'. آن پاک‌دل را is the definite object marked by را ('that pure‑hearted one' as direct object). ز پای literally 'from (his) feet' is an idiom meaning 'to the ground' or 'off his feet'.
segments.s306-g77: سر تاجداری is a genitive/possessive construction meaning 'the head of kingship' (سر as the anatomical 'head' or top of authority). چنان ارجمند is an adjectival phrase following the noun and emphasizing the high worth/estimation ('so/so very precious'). The order (noun then modifier) is typical in Persian poetic phrasing.
segments.s306-g78: بریدی is 2nd‑person past 'you cut/you severed'. به سان is the simile marker 'like/as', and سر گوسفند is the comparison object 'the head of a sheep' (literal image to emphasize how easily or ignobly the head was cut off).
segments.s306-g79: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', گاه 'time/era', so ز گاه منوچهر تا این زمان is the temporal span 'from the era of Manūchehr until this time' with تا introducing the endpoint. The sequence is chronological and uses the usual preposition + noun order.
segments.s306-g80: نبودی is a negated past existential from بودن ('there was not' / 'there has not been'); in classical Persian this form serves impersonally. مگر is the exceptionive 'except' (introducing what is excluded), followed by the coordinated qualities بدتن 'ill‑bodied' and بدگمان 'ill‑dispositioned / suspicious' — together meaning only such bad sorts existed.
segments.s306-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از marks origin 'from'. جهن is a proper name (Jahan) and is the source of the message. پیغام is the noun 'message'. بشنید is the past tense, 3rd-person singular 'he heard'. شاه is the subject 'the king' (appears after the verb in this poetic word order).
segments.s306-g2: همی کرد is a classical/perfective-progressive verbal construction where همی marks continued or habitual action and کرد is the past of 'to do' used here with an adjectival complement. خندان is a predicative adjective 'smiling'. بدو is the allative clitic/phrase 'to him'. بر + نگاه ('بر نگاه') means 'with a gaze/toward (with one's look)'; بر governs نگاه as the direction/instrument of the smiling. The whole phrase therefore describes the king smiling and directing his look at the addressee.
segments.s306-g3: به پاسخ is an adverbial phrase 'in reply/as an answer'. چنین گفت is the fixed expression 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'he said/spoke'); poetic word order places the speech formula before the vocative. کای is the vocative particle 'O', and رزمجوی is the vocative noun 'warrior/combatant'.
segments.s306-g4: شنیدیم is the perfect past 'we/ I heard' (here 'we heard' as a reportive narrator). سر تا سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'from top to top' = 'throughout' or 'from beginning to end' (each سر is the noun 'top/summit' used adverbially). این گفت و گوی 'this conversation' is the object: گفت و گو is a compound meaning 'conversation' (گفت here is part of the compound 'speech/saying' and گوی carries the sense 'saying').
segments.s306-g5: نخست introduces an enumeration 'first'. آن‌که is a nominalized relative 'that which/that (you did)'. کردی is 2nd-person singular past 'you did'. م + را here represent the object clitic 'مرا' = 'me' (split in the segmentation); م is the pronominal element and را is the definite/object marker attached to it, so together mark 'me' as the direct object. آفرین is an exclamation 'bravo' or 'praise' directed at the addressee.
segments.s306-g6: همان is 'that/same' qualifying باد. باد here is the optative/benedictive particle 'may' (a common poetic wish), not literal 'wind'. بر governs the following nouns and means 'upon/on'. تخت ('throne'), تاج ('crown'), and نگین ('gem' or signet) are the objects of the benediction; the list is linked by و 'and'. The phrase therefore expresses a blessing: 'May that very (good) thing be upon throne, crown, and gem.'
segments.s306-g7: درودی is 'a greeting' or 'salutation'. که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'. دادی is 2nd-person singular past 'you gave'. ز (poetic variant of از) means 'from' or 'concerning' and links the object to افراسیاب (Afrasiab), the proper name meaning the greeting concerned Afrasiab.
segments.s306-g8: بگفتی is 2nd-person past 'you said' (the speaker reports what the addressee said). که introduces the reported content. او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. کرد is 'made/caused' (past). مژگان is 'eyelashes' and پر آب is the adjectival phrase 'full of water', where پر means 'full (of)' and آب is 'water' (here a conventional image for tears).
segments.s306-g25: دلت = دل + enclitic -ت (your); the possessor is attached to the noun. جادوی را uses جادو(ی) as the thing being taken/appointed, and را is the direct-object/definiteness marker that here highlights the item made into an asset. چو ... گشت is a simile with گشت (past of گشتن) meaning 'became/turned into' — literally 'your heart became (as) magic's capital/asset.'
segments.s306-g26: سخن بر زبانت = 'words/speech upon your tongue' (سخن + locative/covering preposition بر + زبان + enclitic -ت 'your'). چو پیرایه گشت again uses چو + گشت to mean 'became like an ornament'; پیرایه is a figurative noun 'adornment/embellishment.' Word order is poetic but the relation is 'the speech on your tongue became like ornament.'
segments.s306-g27: زبان پر ز گفتار = 'a tongue full (پر) of speech/words' where پر + ز + noun ('full of') is the usual poetic construction. دل پر دروغ = 'a heart full of falsehoods'; the two noun phrases are linked by و ('and') to parallel each other. Note پر governs the following ز + noun phrase.
segments.s306-g28: بر مرد دانا نگیرد فروغ = a negative statement with نگیرد (present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not take / will not seize'); فروغ (radiance) is the grammatical subject which 'does not take hold on' (بر) a wise man (مرد دانا). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'on a wise man, radiance does not seize.'
segments.s306-g29: پدر کشته را = 'the slain father' with را marking the phrase as the object of the verb. شاه گیتی مخوان uses مخوان as a negative imperative ('do not call/declare'); here شاه گیتی = 'king of the world' is the name/title to be (not) uttered. In Persian poetry negative imperatives are often formed by مخوان (do not call/recite).
segments.s306-g30: کنون کز سیاوش نماند استخوان = 'now that (کز) of Siyavash no bone remains.' کنون = 'now'; کز = 'that/which' introduces the subordinate clause; نماند is past/nearly past 'remains/left' (here negative: none remains); استخوان = 'bones/remains' (i.e., physical remains).
segments.s306-g31: همان مادرم را = 'that very mother of mine' (همان 'that/same' + مادر + enclitic -م 'my'); را marks her as object. ز پرده به راه = 'from the curtain to the road' (ز = from, پرده = curtain/veil, به = to, راه = road/way). The enclitic -م is attached to مادر (مادرم); the provided lemma/senses for the isolated 'م' don't match this possessive suffix, so its sense is not represented in the given list.
segments.s306-g32: کشیدی و گشتی چنین کینه خواه = کشیدی (past of کشیدن) is 'you pulled/dragged.' و = 'and.' گشتی here is intended as past of گشتن 'you became,' but the sense list supplied did not include that 'become' sense for this token (so index omitted); چنین = 'thus/so'; کینه خواه is a compound where خواه ('desirer/seeker') modifies کینه ('hatred, grudge') giving 'one who seeks/harbors a grudge' — functionally an adjectival/nominal description 'became such a grudge-bearer.'
segments.s306-g105: به جنگ + noun marks direction/scene: 'to/into (the) battle (against...)'. The name/word پشن immediately follows as the opponent (here a proper name), then نیز 'also' qualifies the statement. چندان is a quantifier meaning 'so many/such', and سپاه is the governed noun 'army/host' — together 'چندان سپاه' = 'so many troops'. Word order: prepositional phrase (به جنگ پشن) followed by the quantifier phrase.
segments.s306-g106: که introduces a result/relative clause here: 'that/so that'. پیران in context is a proper name (Piran) acting as the agent; بکشت is an archaic past-form meaning 'killed' (the lexical list given does not list this sense, so treated as irregular here). اندر is an older locative preposition 'in/within', and آوردگاه is 'battlefield' — 'که پیران بکشت اندر آوردگاه' = 'such that Piran killed (them) in the battlefield.'
segments.s306-g107: زمین is the subject 'the earth/land' (here 'land/country'); گل شد is a copular/verb phrase 'became a flower' (a poetic metaphor: the ground was turned into a 'flower' by blood). از introduces the cause/reason 'from / because of', خون is 'blood', and گودرزیان names the group 'the Gudarzis' (genitive agent of the blood).
segments.s306-g108: This clause is elliptic/concise: نجویی (a nominal form from جست‌و‌جو) is used here in a poetic sense of 'relief/rescue/any gain to be sought'; جز 'except' restricts possibilities; از marks the source 'from', رنج 'suffering', و 'and', راه 'way/path', زیان 'loss/harm'. Together: 'no relief/benefit except (mere) suffering and the road of loss.'
segments.s306-g109: کنون 'now' sets the time. آمدی is the past tense verb 'you came' (second-person past form); با introduces accompaniment 'with', and هزاران 'thousands' is the quantified noun — repeated as a poetic plural ('هزاران هزار' = 'thousands and thousands'). The verb form آمدی addresses the addressee directly.
segments.s306-g110: ز is the poetic/preclassical form of 'from'. ترکان is the genitive/attributive 'of the Turks' and سوار 'horsemen/riders' names the troop type. از ... در کارزار literally indicates origin/location with از + در: 'from the gate/entrance of the battle' (در here has the sense 'door/gate'). Word order places the group (ترکان سوار) before the locative phrase.
segments.s306-g111: به + آموی gives direction/indirect object: 'to/for (the) uncle' (آموی = paternal uncle). لشکر is 'army' and کشیدی is the past second-person of کشیدن 'you drew/moved' — here 'to draw/march/bring an army' (transitive). The final به جنگ repeats the destination 'to battle'. Overall: 'you brought/drew the uncle's army to battle.'
segments.s306-g112: وز is an archaic/poetic variant (here functioning like از 'from') — the provided senses do not match this usage, so treated as irregular. ایشان 'they/them' is the source group; به پیش من آمد is a compact clause 'came before me' (به پیش = in the presence of / before), and پشنگ is a proper name placed after the verb in typical epic inversion: 'and from them Pashang came before me.'
segments.s306-g113: فرستادیش is an older/poetic form of فرستاد + ش (he sent him), with the object clitic attached; it means “he sent him.” تا introduces a purpose clause here (’so that / in order that’). ببرد is the present-subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person form of بردن (ب‑ + برد) used for the purpose clause “may/should take”; سرم is the object (my head) attached as a possessive -م but is listed separately in the data.
segments.s306-g114: از آن پس is a temporal phrase “from/after that”; از = “from,” آن = “that/then,” پس = “after/then.” تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun. ویران is an adjective/participle meaning “ruined / laid waste,” and کنی is the 2nd‑person present-subjunctive/optative of کردن (’you may/do make’), yielding ‘you will/may lay waste’; کشورم is ‘my country/realm’ (کشور + possessive -م).
segments.s306-g115: جهاندار یزدان is a compound/title ‘the world‑ruler God’ (subject). مرا is the object ‘me’ (here split in the data as م + را: the م is the personal element and را the object marker), and یار گشت is the predicate ‘became an ally/friend’ — گشت is the past ‘became’ and یار is the predicate noun ‘ally.’
segments.s306-g116: سر بخت here forms a nominal phrase meaning roughly ‘the foremost fate / the fate’ (سر = ‘chief/head’, بخت = ‘fate/fortune’) and دشمن is the genitive (’of the enemy’). نگونسار is an adjectival predicate ‘mournful/woeful,’ and گشت is the past ‘became’ — together: ‘the enemy’s fortune became woeful.’
segments.s306-g117: مرا گویی is an address meaning ‘you say/tell me’ (here the data splits مرا into م + را; م represents the personal element ‘me’). اکنون = ‘now’; که introduces the subordinate content clause ‘that’; از تخت تو = ‘from/at your throne’ (از = ‘from/ because of,’ تخت = ‘throne,’ تو = ‘your’).
segments.s306-g118: دل‌افروز is an adjective ‘heart‑cheering/delightful’; و is the coordinating ‘and’; شادانم is ‘I am joyful’ (شادان + copula -م). از بخت تو = ‘because of/thanks to your fortune’ (از = ‘because of/from,’ بخت = ‘fortune,’ تو = ‘your’). The clause is a predicative comment on the prior clause introduced by که.
segments.s306-g119: نگه کن is an imperative ‘look / behold / consider’ (نگه = look/watch, کن = do!); که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause. تا + چون together function to ask/mark degree — ‘to what extent / how’ — and بود is the past copula ‘was.’ باورم is باور + possessive -م ‘my belief/faith’; the clause asks ‘how my faith was.’
segments.s306-g120: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when’; کردارهای تو is ‘your deeds’ (کردارهای = plural ‘deeds/actions,’ تو = ‘your’). یاد آورم is a compound verb ‘I bring to mind / I recall’ (یاد = ‘memory/remembrance,’ آورم = ‘I bring’), so the clause reads ‘when I recall your deeds.’
segments.s306-g17: ترا is the object-form 'to you' (ت + را) topicalizing the addressee. چند خواهی is a present second‑person interrogative/optative: چَند 'how long/how much' + خواهی 'you will/would want' — here meaning 'how long will you crave'. سخن چرب is a noun phrase 'flattering/flowery speech' (چرب used metaphorically). هست is the copula (present/existential) completing the clause.
segments.s306-g18: به دل is a locative phrase 'in (your) heart'. نیستی is the second‑person negative of بودن 'you are not'. پاک و یزدان‌پرست are predicate adjectives modifying the heart (i.e. 'pure and God‑fearing'); word order places the negation before these predicates, giving 'you are not pure and God‑fearing at heart.'
segments.s306-g19: کسی کو functions as 'one who...' (کسی 'someone' + کو used as a relative/archaic 'who'). به دانش 'by/through knowledge' uses به in the sense 'by/with' (poetic). توانگر is the predicate 'wealthy/rich', and بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'someone who was wealthy by knowledge.'
segments.s306-g20: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/than' establishing a comparison. گفتار 'speech' and کردار 'deed' form the compared items, and بهتر بود is 'was better' (past). The clause means 'his conduct/deeds were better than his speech.'
segments.s306-g21: فریدون is the proper name. فرخ here is an epithet 'fortunate/auspicious' modifying him. ستاره 'star' is used metaphorically ('a great/illustrious one'). نگشت is the past negative/negative perfect of گشت 'to become/turn', so 'did not become/was not turned into.'
segments.s306-g22: نه negates the clause. از خاک تیره 'from dark/obscure soil' indicates origin ('تیره' = dark/lowly). همی is an archaic particle adding emphasis/continuity, and برگذشت is a past verb 'passed/emanated/coming forth' (here 'did not come forth from'). Together: 'he did not come from dark soil' (i.e. he was not of lowly origin).
segments.s306-g23: تو گویی functions as 'you say/you seem to say' (addressing the addressee). که introduces the subordinate clause. من بر شوم uses the subjunctive شوم 'may I / I should become' with بر indicating elevation ('to be raised'), and بر سپهر 'onto the heavens' gives the destination — overall 'you say as if I were to rise into the heavens.'
segments.s306-g24: بشستی is the past second‑person 'you sat/you took a seat' (here idiomatically 'you sit' or 'you remain sitting'). بر is the preposition 'upon/on', این‌گونه 'thus/in this manner', از شرم 'from shame' explains the cause, and چهر 'face' names what is affected — 'you sit with your face like this from shame' (i.e. you are sitting, your face showing shame).
segments.s306-g89: بکشتی is the 2nd‑person past perfect/simple of کشتن (“you killed” / “you have slain”). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” تا بوده‌ای here means “since / as long as you have been” with تا + past of بودن used to give a durative/conditional sense. بدتنی is an adjectival noun ‘of bad disposition/ill‑favored’ describing the subject. Together this cluster says “you killed, and since you have been of bad nature…”.
segments.s306-g90: نه (understood at the start of the fragment) negates what follows: “not of Adam.” از marks origin/source (“from/of”), آدم here refers to the prototypical human/Adam (i.e. humankind), the second از begins a contrastive phrase “but from the seed of…,” تخم is a figurative “seed” / origin, and آهرمنی denotes Ahrimanic/demonic origin. The whole segment: “not of Adam, but from the seed of Ahriman.”
segments.s306-g91: کسی‌گر is a conditional formula meaning “if anyone” (کسی + گر ‘if’). بدیهات (plural of بدیه) means “the manifest/obvious things” (here: obvious deeds or acts). گیرد is the present/subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person form “would take / were to take” used in conditional clauses here to mean “were to count.” شمار is the noun “count/number.” Altogether: “If anyone counts/was to count the manifest things…”
segments.s306-g92: فزون آید is a verbless/result clause meaning “it would be more / would increase” (فزون = greater/more; آید = comes/would come). از before گردش here marks a comparison/limit (“more than / beyond”), گردش روزگار = “the turning/rotation (i.e. cycles) of time” or “the course of the world.” So the clause says the number would surpass the revolutions of time.
segments.s306-g93: نهالی is a diminutive/noun meaning “a young shoot / sapling” (here metaphorical). به marks direction/goal and here means “into” or “to,” و دوزخ is the destination “hell.” فرستاده‌ای is the 2nd‑person perfect of فرستادن “you have sent.” The segment literally: “You have sent a sapling into hell.”
segments.s306-g94: نگویی functions as a negative hortative/irrealis here meaning roughly “one would not / you should not say” (from ن + گویی). که introduces the subordinate clause “that ….” از مردمان = “from/among men” (از = of/from; مردمان = people). زاده‌ای is the perfect/passive‑like predicate “you are born/of such birth” (literally ‘you are born of …’). Altogether: “(One) would not say that you are born of men.”
segments.s306-g95: دگر here means “the other / the other matter.” آن‌که introduces a relative clause “that/which,” and گفتی is the remark recalled (“what you said”); the following که begins the quoted content. دیو پلید is the subject phrase “the vile / foul demon.” The whole fronting phrase: “As for the other thing you said, that the vile demon …”
segments.s306-g96: دل و رای من is a coordinated subject “my heart and my counsel/mind” (دل = heart, رای = counsel/opinion; من marks possession). سوی زشتی کشید is the predicate: سوی = toward, زشتی = ugliness / moral foulness, and کشید is the past form of کشیدن here meaning “drew / was drawn / turned.” Together: “my heart and counsel were drawn toward ugliness.”
segments.s306-g81: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from'. تور (Tūr) is a proper name (the son of Fereydun). اندر آمد is a light verb construction meaning 'came into being / came' with اندر adding a locative/introductory flavour and آمد in simple past/preterite sense. زیان 'harm/detriment' is the noun head and از نخست 'from the first' (= 'from the outset') qualifies the temporal origin of that harm.
segments.s306-g82: کجا is an interrogative 'where' used rhetorically. با is the instrumental/associative preposition 'with'. پدر is 'father'; دست بد را is a noun phrase 'the evil/wicked hand' with را marking it as the definite/direct object. بشست is 3rd person past 'washed' (ب + شست here is the classical past stem), so the clause literally says 'where did (he) wash the evil hand with (his) father?'.
segments.s306-g83: پسر بر پسر is a repetitive construction 'son upon son' indicating succession; بر here marks the directional/relational sense 'upon/over'. بگذرد is a classical 3rd-person present/imperfect form meaning 'passes / succeeds' used generically. همچنین is an adverb 'likewise/similarly' linking to the next clause.
segments.s306-g84: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. راه بزرگی literally 'the way/path of greatness' (راه head noun + بزرگی as qualification). آیین دین 'the ritual/custom of religion' is a nominal phrase (آیین qualifies دین); the copula is omitted in classical style, so the clause denies the existence/observance of these things.
segments.s306-g85: زدی is the 2nd-person singular past 'you struck' (classical past form). گردن نوذر نامدار is the object phrase 'the neck of Nowzar, renowned' with نوذر in genitive relation to گردن and نامدار as a postpositive adjective 'famed/renowned'.
segments.s306-g86: پدر شاه is 'father of the king' (noun-noun genitive relation). وز تخمه شهریار is a poetic/genitive phrase giving lineage: تخمه شهریار 'the seed/offspring of Shahryar'. وز here functions as a linking/formal particle in the poem (an archaic/poetic marker), not the literal senses listed for the lemma in the modern lexicon; the phrase as a whole names Nowzar as scion/ancestor in the royal line.
segments.s306-g87: برادرت is 'your brother' (برادر + 2nd-person suffix -ت). اغریرث is a proper name (here rendered Aghrirath). نیکخوی is an epithet 'good‑natured / kindly‑bred' placed after the name, describing him.
segments.s306-g88: کجا is the interrogative 'where' used rhetorically. نیکنامی 'good name / good repute' is the noun being questioned (the clause omits an explicit copula). بدش آرزوی is a compact passive/impersonal expression meaning 'evil was wished upon him' (بد 'evil/bad' + the enclitic pronoun ش 'to/for him' + آرزوی 'wish'), i.e. people wished him ill.
segments.s306-g121: از این پس (literally “from this after”) is a fixed temporal phrase meaning “from now on.” مرا is the object pronoun ‘me’ (here written as the enclitic sequence م + را); جز + به + NP marks an exception introduced by a preposition: جز به شمشیر تیز = “except by the sharp sword.” This whole segment sets up a temporal/exceptional frame; the main verbal idea appears in the next segment.
segments.s306-g122: نباشد here is a negative existential/stative verb in literary Persian meaning “there will be none” / “there shall be no …”; it functions as the predicate. سخن (speech, talk) is the noun governed by that negation, and با تو = “with you” is an oblique complement. تا رستخیز is a temporal adjunct “until the Resurrection/ uprising,” closing the clause. Word order is typical Persian: (negated) verb-line followed by its noun/complements.
segments.s306-g123: بکوشم is a 1st-person present-subjunctive/jussive form meaning “I will strive” or “I shall try”; the -م ending marks the speaker. به نیروی گنج و سپاه is an instrumental phrase introduced by به (“by/with”), where نیروی = “the power/force of” and گنج و سپاه list the sources (treasure and army). Verb precedes or accompanies these instruments in Persian narrative style.
segments.s306-g124: This is a parallel instrumental phrase: به + (نیک اختر) + و + (گردش هور و ماه). نیک اختر = “a favorable/auspicious star” (نیک used as ‘auspicious’), and گردش here means “the turning / revolution / course” (of the heavenly bodies). هور و ماه = sun (figurative splendor) and moon; the sequence reinforces the cosmic forces invoked as support.
segments.s306-g125: همان پیش یزدان بباشم is a compact clause: پیش یزدان = “in the presence of God / before God,” and بباشم is a 1st-person subjunctive/optative of بودن meaning “that I be / I shall be / I will remain.” به پای attaches as a locative/complement meaning “at the (divine) foot” (i.e. humbly at God’s feet). Word order places the locative after the verb phrase in this poetic register.
segments.s306-g126: نخواهم here is the negated 1st-person form of خواستن and functions as ‘I will not / I shall not have’ (in older usage it negates possession/expectation). به گیتی = “in the world,” جز او = “except him,” and رهنمای = “guide.” Together: “I will have no guide in the world except him.” Word order: verb (نخواهم) followed by its locative and exception phrase.
segments.s306-g127: مگر کز = “unless/from which” (مگر introduces an exception/condition here: “unless”), and بدان is a deictic/relational expression meaning “by/through that” (built from ب + دان in the segment split). پاک گردد is a standard literary passive/resultative construction meaning “becomes pure/clean,” with گردد = ‘becomes.’ جهان is the subject “the world.” The clause therefore gives a conditional purpose/result.
segments.s306-g128: به داد و دهش من is an instrumental/authorial phrase: به داد (“by/with my justice”) و دهش (“and my giving / generosity”) من = “my.” ببندم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive/jussive of بستن (to bind/close) meaning “I will bind/close,” and میان = “between / the middle.” Together: “I will, by my justice and giving, bind/close the middle.” In context this expresses a decisive corrective action unless the world is purified by the thing mentioned above.
segments.s306-g57: به پیش تو is a prepositional phrase 'before you / in your presence' (به + پیش + تو). آ‌ورد is 3rd-person past 'brought' (subject omitted from the line, supplied by context). و is the conjunction 'and', and کردی is the 2nd-person past 'you did/made' used with نگاه (a light inversion of the more common نگاه کردی) to mean 'you cast a look'.
segments.s306-g58: که introduces a content clause 'that...'. هستم is the 1st-person present 'I am' (classical Persian often uses present where English uses past sense), سزاوار is an adjective 'deserving / fit for', and تخت و کلاه are the things of which he claims worthiness (throne and crown/headgear).
segments.s306-g59: به سان 'like / as' (به + سان) introduces a comparison with Siawush. سرم is 'my head' (سر + enclitic -م = my); را is the definite/object marker attaching to سرم. ز (poetic form of از) + تن = 'from the body'. Word order places the object phrase before the verb that follows.
segments.s306-g60: ببری here is a 2nd-person past/subjunctive/optative form 'you cut / may you cut' (classical lines often use this form to express a commanded or hypothetical action). و connects the clauses. تن هم 'the body also' (هم = also/even). نیابد is negative 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'does not / will not find' and کفن is the object 'shroud'.
segments.s306-g61: زبان مرا = 'my tongue' (زبان + enclitic ـم plus object marker را). پاک here functions adverbially 'utterly / completely'. یزدان is the subject 'God / the divine', and ببست is 3rd-person past 'closed / shut' — literally 'God shut my tongue completely', i.e., he was silenced.
segments.s306-g62: همان 'thereupon / then' (here functioning as a discourse adverb). خیره ماندم is 'I remained astonished / dumbfounded' (خیره = astonished/staring; ماندم = I remained). به جای نشست = 'sat down in (his/my) place' (به جای = in place; نشست = sat). The two verbs (ماندم ... نشست) share the same speaker-subject.
segments.s306-g63: مرا is the object marker 'me' (here split as enclitic + را in the word list). بی دل و بی‌خرد is a coordinated description 'without heart and without sense' = 'heartless and foolish'. یافتی is 2nd-person past 'you found' (you perceived / judged me as such).
segments.s306-g64: به کردار بد = 'into bad conduct / to an evil deed' (به = toward/in, کردار = deed/behavior, بد = bad). تیز نشتافتی is a 2nd-person past 'you hastened swiftly' (تیز = swiftly/keenly; نشتافتی < شتافتن = you hurried/rushed). The clause says you promptly rushed into evil action.
segments.s306-g9: شنیدم is a past tense first‑person verb 'I heard' (here: 'I have heard' / 'I learned'). همین باد is a noun phrase 'this very wind/breeze' (همین = 'this very', باد used poetically to mean a wind of fortune or as the subject of an ensuing wish). بر تاج و تخت is a prepositional phrase (بر + noun phrase) meaning 'upon the crown and throne' and follows the noun it modifies in this poetic word order.
segments.s306-g10: مبادم here is an optative/modal classical form expressing a wish ('may it be' / 'may it come to pass'); in older Persian such optative forms are used to express blessings or hopes. مگر introduces the wished outcome/condition and here connects that wish to the following adjectives (i.e. 'so that'); شاد و پیروزبخت are coordinate adjectives 'happy and fortunate/prosperous' describing the hoped‑for state.
segments.s306-g11: دوم = 'second' (ordinal adverb introducing the second point). آن‌که begins a clause 'that which / the thing that' and here links to what follows; گفتی in ordinary Persian would be the past tense 'you said', but the supplied senses list does not include that verbal sense (so the correct sense is not among the supplied options). ز یزدان سپاس is a poetic phrasing 'thanks to God' or 'gratitude toward God' (ز = 'from/for' in classical usage, یزدان = 'God', سپاس = 'thanks').
segments.s306-g12: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'). بینم is a subjunctive/present form 'I may see' or 'may I behold' expressing desire or purpose after که. همی is a classical particle often giving durative or emphatic shading ('ever/always' or 'indeed'); here it emphasizes the wish. پور یزدان‌شناس is a noun phrase 'a son (پور) who knows God / a God‑knowing son' (یزدان‌شناس = a pious or God‑knowing person).
segments.s306-g13: ز شاهان گیتی is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of/from the kings of the world' (ز used poetically as 'of' or 'from'). دل افروزتر is a comparative adjective 'more heart‑delighting' (دل‑افروز = 'heart‑pleasing'); in Persian poetry comparatives often precede or are juxtaposed with the domain they exceed.
segments.s306-g14: پسندیده‌تر is another comparative 'more pleasing / more beloved' modifying the implied ruler; شاه is the noun 'king' (the adjective+noun order is a poetic inversion). و connects the next quality; پیروزتر is comparative 'more victorious' or 'more triumphant'. Together they stack comparative praises of the same referent.
segments.s306-g15: This line is elliptical/poetic: مرا (me) is the accusative object, here split in the provided tokens as م + را; داد is the past‑tense verb 'gave' with یزدان as subject 'God gave'. همه هرچه گفت is a relative clause 'all (i.e. everything) that he said' (هرچه ... گفت = 'whatever he said'); the verb گفت appears at the clause end as is normal in Persian, but the provided senses for گفت do not include the simple past 'he said' and so are not matched in the list.
segments.s306-g16: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'that' or 'so that'. با این هنرها is a prepositional phrase 'with these arts/skills' (با = 'with', این = 'these/the present', هنرها = 'arts/skills/virtues'). خرد is the noun 'wisdom' functioning as the semantic subject of the wish; باد is the classical optative particle 'may it be' (a wish), and جفت here means 'paired/matched' (i.e. 'may wisdom be paired with these arts'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: subject (خرد) + optative (باد) + predicate (جفت).
segments.s306-g65: سیاوش نگه کن is an imperative address: سیاوش (proper name) + نگه کن = “look/heed” (نگه here functions as ‘look/gaze’ with the imperative کن). که introduces a subordinate clause (’that/for’), and از راستی is a prepositional phrase (‘from/out of honesty/uprightness’) that will attach to the action named in the following clause.
segments.s306-g66: چه کرد و چه دید contains two parallel past-tense 3rd‑person predicates: کرد (‘did’) and دید (‘saw’), each introduced by the interrogative/indefinite چه (‘what’). از بد و کاستی is a prepositional complement (‘of/from bad [things] and deficiency/shortcomings’) that links to the second verb (چه دید) and contrasts with از راستی earlier.
segments.s306-g67: ز گیتی بیامد is a prepositional/time phrase + past verb: ز گیتی (‘from the world’) + بیامد (‘came’). ترا is the 2nd‑person object (here written as ت + را for metrical reasons) and برگزید is the past 3rd‑person verb ‘chose/selected’ (object pronoun precedes the verb here for emphasis/poetry).
segments.s306-g68: چنان کز ره نامداران سزید = result clause introduced by چنان که (‘such that’). کز is the relative/contracted form of که + ز, introducing ‘from/among the way of the renowned’; سزید is an archaic/past-form of سزا ‘to deserve’ meaning ‘was deserving / befitted’ and refers back to the person addressed (implicit from ترا).
segments.s306-g69: ز بهر تو = ‘for your sake’ (prepositional phrase). پرداخت آیین is a verbal phrase in the past tense: پرداخت here means ‘carried out / performed’ and آیین is ‘rite/ceremony’; و گاه (‘and at a time’ / ‘and then’) sets up the temporal frame for the following action.
segments.s306-g70: بیامد ز گیتی is again ‘came from the world’ (past intransitive). ترا خواند شاه = object (ترا) + verb خواند (‘called/summoned’) + subject (شاه) placed after the verb for emphasis — a common poetic inversion: ‘the king called/summoned you.’ خواند here is in the sense ‘to call/summon.’
segments.s306-g71: وفا جست is a terse poetic past: وفا ‘fidelity/faithfulness’ + جست (from جستن) meaning here ‘sought’ or ‘showed’ (i.e., strove for fidelity). و بگذاشت آن انجمن: بگذاشت (past of گذاشتن) in this context means ‘left/abandoned’ and آن انجمن is ‘that assembly/council’ — the verb + object order is poetic but straightforward.
segments.s306-g72: بدان تا = a purposive link: ب (prefix) + دان (here functioning as ‘with/for that’ or ‘so that one may know’), تا = ‘so that/in order that’. نخوانیش is a negated verbal phrase meaning ‘they would not call/name him’ (ن‑ + خوانِد + ـش/یش for object), and پیمان‌شکن is the predicate noun ‘oath‑breaker’ — together: ‘so that they should not call him an oath‑breaker.’
segments.s306-g33: This phrase names the thing/agent that causes the speaker's suffering: نوز نازاده 'newborn' + از مادرم 'from my mother'. The initial مرا appears here as a cliticized topical/object element (classical word order allows such clitics to precede the noun phrase); syntactically the whole unit supplies the reason/source for the action in the next segment.
segments.s306-g34: همی + افروختی is a classical/poetic verbal phrase: همی marks continued or emphatic action and افروختی is 2nd person singular past (you kindled). آتش is the direct object 'fire' and بر سرم ('on my head') gives the affected location; together it means 'you kindled a fire upon my head' (i.e. caused great suffering).
segments.s306-g35: هر آن کس که = 'whoever/anyone who' (هر آن کس with the relative که). او is an emphatic pronoun 'he/that one', بد is the predicate/adjective 'bad' and بدرگاه تو = 'in your presence/at your court' (بدرگاه = presence/at court). Word order follows classical Persian flexibility: relative phrase + emphatic pronoun + predicate + locative phrase.
segments.s306-g36: بنفرید is an older/poetic verb form here meaning an action of harming or wronging (the exact dictionary sense in the supplied list doesn't match; the clause as a whole reads 'does wrong/inflicts (بنفرید) upon (بر) جان = life/soul of ...'. بی‌راه تو qualifies that action as 'unwarranted/unjust' (بی‌راه = without right).
segments.s306-g37: که introduces a clause giving an emphatic statement. هرگز = 'never'; به گیتی = 'in the world' (به with گیتی, world); کس = 'anyone'; آن بد = 'that bad/that evil'; نکرد = negative past 'did not do'. The verb نَکرد governs the negation for the whole clause.
segments.s306-g38: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the classes listed: شاهان 'kings', گردان 'troops/retinues', و مردان مرد a doubled epithet meaning 'and the men of men' i.e. the bravest/most valiant men. The sequence enumerates who never did such a thing.
segments.s306-g39: که again introduces a clause describing the act. بر انجمن = 'into/onto the assembly' (بر used with مكان in classical style), مر here is the clitic 'me' or 'to me' (classical accusative/dative clitic), زنی را کشان = 'dragging a woman' where زنی را is the object (را marks definiteness/emphasis) and کشان is the present-participial/gerundial form 'dragging'. Word order places the clitic before the participial phrase.
segments.s306-g40: سپارد is 'he/they entrust/hand over' (past or timeless statement in narrative). بزرگی = 'greatness/authority' is the thing given, به مردم = 'to the people' (here 'to those men'), and کشان again describes those people as 'the draggers' (i.e. men who drag others). The clause says that greatness is entrusted/given to the men who drag people.

sentences.s306-s65: I will cut the malicious one out from the midst, and I will make their heads helmetless.
sentences.s306-s66: Tell Niyâ whatever words I have said; in battle do not seek many excuses.
sentences.s306-s49: Zahhak spoke the same, and Jamshid as well, when their hearts became hopeless of goodness.
sentences.s306-s50: for the devil led our heart astray and cut us off from every goodness.
sentences.s306-s51: Ill fate did not turn away from them; 'ill fortune comes from a bad essence,' said the teacher.
sentences.s306-s52: Whoever turns his head away from righteousness will choose crookedness and deficiency.
sentences.s306-s21: The one who struck kept lashing with the whip, so that his daughter might cast away the child.
sentences.s306-s22: The wise elder reached that place and saw what he had never seen or heard.
sentences.s306-s23: Thus was the command of God: that I should be exalted in every assembly.
sentences.s306-s24: He made your harm and calamity come from me, for Time had one secret in common with me.
sentences.s306-s25: From the time I was parted from my mother, I was like a wretched child.
sentences.s306-s26: We sent (him/me) to the shepherds; we gave (him) to stout/brave men to be reared.
sentences.s306-s27: They gave me a nurse and a shepherds’ attendant; there was neither the day’s rest nor the shepherd’s sleep.
sentences.s306-s28: Thus it was until my day (my time/fortune) passed; elders from the plain brought me in.
sentences.s306-s37: When you saw upon and around it the neck — its size, its roundness, and the path to it.
sentences.s306-s38: That ill jewel moved from its place and threw the pure‑hearted one from his feet.
sentences.s306-s39: You severed the head of kingship so noble, as one severs the head of a sheep.
sentences.s306-s40: From the time of Manūchehr until this time there has been none except the ill‑bodied and the ill‑disposed.
sentences.s306-s1: When the king heard the message from Jahan, he smiled and looked toward him.
sentences.s306-s2: In answer he thus spoke: 'O warrior,'
sentences.s306-s3: First: that you praised me — may that same wind be upon throne, crown, and gem.
sentences.s306-s4: The greeting that you gave to Afrasiab — you said that he made his eyelashes full of water.
sentences.s306-s13: Your heart made magic its treasure; the words upon your tongue became like an ornament.
sentences.s306-s14: A tongue full of words and a heart full of lies — radiance will not seize a wise man.
sentences.s306-s15: Do not call the slain father 'king of the world' now that not even a bone of Siyavash remains.
sentences.s306-s16: You dragged that very mother of mine from behind the curtain into the road, and so became such a seeker of revenge.
sentences.s306-s53: Against Pashang in battle there were likewise so many troops that Piran killed them on the field.
sentences.s306-s54: The earth became a flower from the blood of the Gudarzis; no succour was to be found except suffering and the road of ruin.
sentences.s306-s55: Now you came with thousands upon thousands, horsemen of the Turks, from the gate of the battlefield.
sentences.s306-s56: You drew your uncle's army into battle, and from them Pashang came before me.
sentences.s306-s57: He sent him to take my head; thereafter you will ravage my country.
sentences.s306-s58: The world-ruler God became my ally; the enemy’s fortune turned woeful.
sentences.s306-s59: You tell me now that, from your throne, I am heart‑cheered and joyful by your fortune.
sentences.s306-s60: Look (consider), for when I recall your deeds, see how my faith stood.
sentences.s306-s9: How long will you desire flattering words? You do not have a pure, God‑fearing heart.
sentences.s306-s10: Someone who was wealthy through learning; his deeds were better than his words.
sentences.s306-s11: Fereydun did not become a fortunate star; he did not emerge from dark soil.
sentences.s306-s12: You say as if I should be raised to the heavens; you sit with your face thus from shame.
sentences.s306-s45: You have slain (them), and since you have always been of an evil temperament, you are not of Adam but sprung from Ahriman's seed.
sentences.s306-s46: If anyone were to count the manifest deeds, the tally would exceed the revolutions (the course) of time.
sentences.s306-s47: You have sent a young shoot into hell; one would not say that you were born among men.
sentences.s306-s48: As for the other thing you said — that the vile demon drew my heart and counsel toward ugliness.
sentences.s306-s41: From Tūr came harm from the very beginning; where did he wash his evil hand with his father?
sentences.s306-s42: One son succeeds another; likewise, there is neither the path of greatness nor the observance of religion.
sentences.s306-s43: You struck the neck of Nowzar the famed, the kingly father and scion of the house of Shahryar.
sentences.s306-s44: Your brother Aghrirath, of good disposition — where is his good repute? Evil was wished upon him.
sentences.s306-s61: From now on I will have no speech with you except by the sharp sword until the Resurrection.
sentences.s306-s62: I will strive with the strength of treasure and an army, by an auspicious star and the revolutions of sun and moon.
sentences.s306-s63: I will remain before God at (His) feet; I will have no guide in the world except Him.
sentences.s306-s64: Unless thereby the world becomes purified, I will, by my justice and generosity, bind (shut) the middle.
sentences.s306-s29: They brought me before you, and you cast a glance, thinking that I was deserving of the throne and crown.
sentences.s306-s30: Like Siavash, cut off my head from my body — and the body still will not find a shroud.
sentences.s306-s31: God shut my tongue clean; stunned, I remained and sat down in my place.
sentences.s306-s32: You found me heartless and senseless, and you swiftly rushed into bad deeds.
sentences.s306-s5: I have heard that this very breeze may fall upon the crown and throne, so that you may be happy and fortunate.
sentences.s306-s6: Second, that you gave thanks to God; that I may always behold a God‑knowing son.
sentences.s306-s7: More heart‑cheering than the kings of the world, more beloved — a king and more victorious.
sentences.s306-s8: God gave me all that he said; may wisdom be paired with these arts.
sentences.s306-s33: Siyâvash, take heed: what he did from uprightness and what he saw of evil and shortcomings.
sentences.s306-s34: He came from the world and chose you, so that you were deemed worthy among the renowned.
sentences.s306-s35: For your sake he performed the rites, and at one time he came from the world — the king summoned you.
sentences.s306-s36: He sought fidelity and left that assembly, so that they would not call him an oath-breaker.
sentences.s306-s17: By taking my newborn from my mother, you have kindled a fire upon my head.
sentences.s306-s18: Whoever behaves badly in your presence does unjust harm to your life.
sentences.s306-s19: For never in the world did anyone do that evil — neither among kings, nor among troops, nor among the bravest of men.
sentences.s306-s20: — to drag a woman into the assembly and to bestow greatness on those who drag people.
